nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater streams and forest pools in the Ucayali drainage of Peru, where the water is densely shaded, tannin-stained, and very soft, with a substrate of fine sand, leaf litter, and submerged roots.

decorPreferences: caves (terracotta pots, coconut huts, ceramic caves)
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, stem plants)
blackwater tannin staining (peat filtration or almond leaves)

diet.notes: In the wild, the Panduro Dwarf Cichlid is a micropredator that feeds on small insects, crustaceans, worms, and zooplankton. In the aquarium it generally accepts a range of frozen and live foods, including baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, bloodworms, and white worms. High-quality small-format pellets or gel foods are also taken by conditioned fish. A varied diet that includes live or frozen items is recommended, particularly when conditioning a pair for breeding.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetras, rummynose tetras)
avoidWith: other Apistogramma males without large territory separation
boisterous fast-moving fish that outcompete for food
large shrimp (fry risk during brood care)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Cisamarc; 2091x1569. Tack-sharp adult male showing the diagnostic intense electric-blue iridescent body, black lateral bar, yellow-green fins and red-and-black margined caudal fin — the strongest, most vibrant shot.
Photo by MGorges; 2205x1609. Adult male with sky-blue iridescence and red-margined caudal fin; whole fish in frame but focus is slightly soft, so kept as a secondary.
Photo by MGorges; 1657x1169. Cryptic yellow-brown female showing the nijsseni-group three-spot panda pattern; illustrates sexual dimorphism. Slightly soft with some algae film, so non-recommended.

summary: The Panduro Dwarf Cichlid (Apistogramma panduro) is a Peruvian blackwater species in which males develop an intense electric-blue body with yellow-and-black fin markings. It is closely related to A. nijsseni and requires soft, acidic water with cave spawning sites to do well.
careNotes:
The Panduro Dwarf Cichlid was described by Römer & Hahn in 1995 and is endemic to the Ucayali drainage of Peru, where it inhabits shallow, heavily shaded blackwater streams and floodplain pools. Males are among the more visually striking dwarf cichlids available in the hobby: the body is suffused with intense electric-blue or sky-blue iridescence, contrasting with a black lateral stripe and vivid yellow-and-black dorsal and caudal fin markings. Females are substantially smaller and cryptically yellow-brown, carrying a three-spot 'panda' pattern — a dark lateral blotch, dorsal spot, and caudal peduncle spot — shared with related species in the nijsseni group.

Apistogramma panduro is closely related to A. nijsseni [fw-fish-205]. The two species differ principally in geographic range — A. panduro is found in the Ucayali drainage, while A. nijsseni occurs in the Loreto region near Iquitos — as well as in subtle differences in female coloration. The two should not be kept together, as they may hybridize.

Soft, acidic, tannin-stained blackwater is strongly preferred, with pH 5.0–6.5 and very low hardness (gH 1–6). Hard or alkaline tap water tends to cause chronic stress and a shortened lifespan; in hard-water areas, RO water reconstituted with a blackwater conditioner and appropriate pH buffering is generally advisable. A temperature range of 24–29°C is appropriate, with 26–28°C commonly considered optimal for breeding activity.

A well-decorated aquarium of at least 80 L with a minimum 80 cm footprint is recommended. Multiple caves — ceramic pots, terracotta tubes, or coconut-shell huts — provide essential spawning sites and retreat points. A layer of Indian almond (Terminalia catappa) leaves along with driftwood or twisted roots recreates the natural environment, leaches beneficial tannins, and provides additional shelter. Sandy substrate is preferred.

In nature this species is haremic, meaning one male associates with and defends a territory that overlaps those of several females. In the aquarium, one male may be kept with two to three females provided the tank is large enough for each female to hold her own small area around an individual cave. A single pair (one male, one female) is also workable in smaller tanks. Males tend to be territorial toward other males and should not be housed together without very large, heavily decorated tanks that offer genuine visual separation.

The Panduro Dwarf Cichlid is generally compatible with small, peaceful community fish that occupy the upper water zones: cardinal and rummynose tetras, pencilfish, otocinclus, and corydoras catfish are classic companions in a biotope-style setup. Boisterous or fin-nipping species that outcompete at feeding time are best avoided.
breedingNotes:
Cave spawner with strong maternal brood care, characteristic of the genus. The female selects a cave, deposits 40–80 eggs on the interior ceiling, and vigorously defends the clutch and subsequent fry from all tankmates, including the male. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 27°C; larvae become free-swimming 5–7 days later. The female leads and guards the fry school for several weeks.

Breeding is triggered by warm, soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.2, gH 1–4) and conditioning with live or frozen foods. A small water change with slightly cooler, very soft water mimics the rainy-season trigger common to Peruvian Apistogramma. In haremic setups, multiple females may spawn simultaneously with the male. The male should be monitored: if he harasses the guarding female excessively in smaller tanks, temporary separation is advisable. Fry initially accept freshly hatched Artemia nauplii (baby brine shrimp) and infusoria; micro-pellets can be introduced after 1–2 weeks. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to water chemistry requirements, though the species is more tractable than some blackwater Apistogramma once suitable conditions are achieved.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma panduro
primary.notes: Primary husbandry and taxonomy reference. Provided native range (Ucayali drainage, Peru), water parameters (pH 4.5–6.5, hardness <5 dH, temperature 22–28°C), adult size (males to 7 cm TL, females to 4 cm), habitat description, breeding, and diet notes. Taxonomy: Cichliformes/Cichlidae per modern classification.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma panduro
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichliformes, Cichlidae), native range (Ucayali River basin, Peru), max size (male ~8 cm TL per morphological records), water parameters, and species description date (Römer & Hahn, 1995). Confirms close relationship with A. nijsseni within the nijsseni species group.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Dwarf Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma husbandry guide; cross-checked tank size (20 gal minimum for pairs), water parameters (soft acidic water emphasis), diet (live and frozen foods), haremic social structure, and cave spawning behavior as applicable to A. panduro. No panduro-specific page available.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Apistogramma species profiles
additional.notes: Cross-checked nijsseni-group characteristics (female 3-spot panda pattern, blackwater requirements, cave spawning), geographic separation between panduro (Ucayali) and nijsseni (Loreto/Iquitos), and male blue coloration as species-defining field mark. Breeding temperature and pH triggers confirmed.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Dwarf Cichlids of Peru
additional.notes: Referenced for geographic range confirmation, nijsseni-group phylogenetic placement, and field collection notes regarding blackwater habitat characteristics (low conductivity, heavy leaf litter, dense canopy shading) in Ucayali tributaries where A. panduro was originally collected.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner with strong maternal brood care, producing clutches of 40–80 eggs. Breeding is triggered by soft, acidic blackwater (pH 5.5–6.2) and conditioning with live or frozen foods. The female guards the clutch and leads the fry school; the male should be monitored and may need to be separated in smaller tanks if he harasses the brooding female.
